notes.anchor: Antony reached Forum Iulii with his leading troops on the Ides of May
notes.note: The Ides of May = 15 May 43 BC. Forum Iulii (modern Fréjus) lay on the coastal road by which Antony, retreating from Mutina, was moving west toward Lepidus's province.

notes.anchor: Ventidius is two days' march behind him
notes.note: P. Ventidius Bassus, a Caesarian general of humble origins (Plancus later sneers at him as a muleteer, Fam. 10.18.3), was bringing three legions to reinforce Antony after the defeat at Mutina.

notes.anchor: Lepidus has his camp at Forum Voconii, a place twenty-four miles from Forum Iulii
notes.note: Forum Voconii was a posting-station on the Via Aurelia in Narbonese Gaul, between Fréjus and Aquae Sextiae (Aix); Lepidus's choice to halt and "wait" there foreshadows his reluctance to engage Antony.

notes.anchor: after the terrible loss of the consuls
notes.note: Both consuls of 43 BC, A. Hirtius and C. Vibius Pansa, died in the Mutina campaign, leaving Rome without its chief magistrates; Plancus sends his ailing brother home to fill the gap in civic administration.

notes.anchor: required such a citizen as praetor for civic duties
notes.note: Plancus's brother, sent back to Rome, held a praetorship in 43 BC; with the consuls dead, praetors carried the burden of the city's magisterial business (urbana officia).

notes.anchor: he sent Apella to me, so that I should hold him as a hostage
notes.note: Apella, an agent (probably a freedman) of Lepidus, was handed over as a personal guarantee (obses) of Lepidus's good faith in the alliance Plancus believed he had secured; Lepidus's defection to Antony soon nullified the pledge.

notes.anchor: L. Gellius gave me proof of his zeal
notes.note: L. Gellius Publicola, who served Plancus here as an intermediary to Lepidus; the same man is later remembered as a turncoat who deserted the Liberators. The Latin of this clause is corrupt (daggered), and the words "from the three brothers Segavianus" are unintelligible.
